text: Rockburst, in terms of its fracture mechanism, is a type of brittle failure or explosion caused by the sudden release of elastic strain energy in rock under excavational unloading conditions. The rock fragments (pieces) caused by the explosion can detach from the parent body through various mechanisms such as explosive dislodgment, explosive spalling, or explosive ejection, with the detachment method, initial velocity, and size being influenced by numerous factors including the fracture mechanism during the explosion and the amount of released elastic strain energy as well as the impacted depth. Rockbursts often occur in tunnels at depths of 500 to 1000 meters or more, but in highly compressed zones, such as the Qinling area where the tunnel depth is around 100 to 400 meters (with the northern entrance approximately 100 meters deep), rockbursts can also occur, posing serious hazards. For instance, the Ooshimizu Tunnel (22.221 km) experienced severe rockbursts in quartz diorite; the New Ooshimizu Tunnel (13.500 km) had rockbursts lasting 2 to 3 months, ejecting rock pieces measuring between 0.2x0.2 m and 1.2x2.5 m with thicknesses of 0.05 to 1.0 m; the Kan'etsu Tunnel, also in quartz diorite, experienced 1433 rockbursts (1417 at the face, 16 on the side walls), with one rockburst ejecting a rock piece that traveled 45 meters. In China, the maximum depth of 1650 meters is at the Guancun Dam Tunnel (6.178 km) in siliceous limestone, where severe rockbursts have occurred, with ejection distances of 2 to 3 meters and a maximum ejected rock size of 0.5x0.4x0.07 m. Major hydropower station diversion tunnels such as Dajingqiao, Taipingxi, Ertan, and Norway's Sima have also experienced varying degrees of rockbursts. Intense rockbursts pose a significant threat to personnel and machinery.

The earliest recorded rock bursts in the world occurred in the 1830s at the Chabisek coal mine in the UK, while the earliest documented rock burst in China took place in 1993 at the Fushun Shengli coal mine. The rock ejection phenomenon that occurred during the construction of the aqueduct tunnel in New York City in the 1950s may represent the earliest record of a tunnel rock burst. The Norwegian Hegura Road Tunnel and the Vesta aqueduct tunnel in Sweden are typical examples of tunnel rock bursts. Numerous hydropower and tunnel projects both domestically and internationally have recorded instances of rock bursts. In China, the Guancun Dam Tunnel of the Chengdu-Kunming Railway line, with a maximum depth of 1650m, is composed of siliceous limestone and has experienced significant rock bursts, with ejection distances of 2-3m and maximum ejected block dimensions of 0.5x0.4x0.07m. The most typical cases of tunnel rock bursts in the country include the Erlang Mountain Highway Tunnel, the Tianshengqiao Hydropower Station aqueduct tunnel, the underground powerhouse of the Ertan Hydropower Station, and the Qinling Tunnel. In Japan, the Niikiyomizu Tunnel (13.500km) experienced rock bursts lasting 2-3 months, with ejected rock block sizes ranging from 0.2x0.2m to 1.2x2.5m, and thicknesses of 0.05 to 1.0m. The Kanetsu Tunnel, composed of quartz diorite, has recorded 1433 rock bursts (1417 at the face and 16 at the side walls), with one instance ejecting a rock block of 45m.

Rockburst is a dynamic mechanical phenomenon that occurs during the excavation of underground engineering, whereby brittle rock masses release stored elastic strain energy rapidly due to the disturbance caused by blasting operations under high-stress conditions. This results in the fracturing, loosening, spalling, or ejection of surrounding rock in the excavated space. The mechanism of rockburst formation is inherently linked to the underlying principles governing its occurrence, allowing for the identification of the causes, conditions, and effects associated with rockbursts. 

Classification of rockbursts is one of the primary bases for prediction and management. There are two main criteria for rockburst classification: one is based on the characteristics of the rockburst, and the other is based on the stress application method involved. The key features of rockbursts include the following aspects: (1) the surrounding rock is typically hard and brittle, with the uniaxial compressive strength of the burst-affected rock mass exceeding 50 MPa. During a rockburst, blocks of rock are expelled rapidly from the excavated walls, usually exhibiting irregular shapes that are thicker in the middle and thinner at the edges, with sizes often being a few centimeters in length and width, and occasionally reaching lengths of tens of centimeters. In severe cases, the entire face can be damaged instantaneously; (2) the occurrence of rockbursts is not necessarily related to the burial depth of the underground engineering, as they commonly occur around 700 to 800 meters deep, although instances have been recorded at depths of around 100 meters; (3) rockbursts mainly occur in Class II and III surrounding rock; they do not occur in very intact rock masses, nor in Class IV or V surrounding rock with significant fracturing. The rock mass in areas where rockburst occurs tends to be relatively dry, and areas with groundwater or fractures typically do not experience rockbursts; (4) the occurrence of rockbursts is somewhat related to the construction method; under equivalent geological conditions, rockbursts are less frequent when using tunneling machines as opposed to conventional mining methods.

Rock bursts, as a complex dynamic phenomenon, exhibit suddenness and can vary in terms of destruction severity, occurrence scale, and timing under different conditions. The main characteristics of rock bursts include: (1) Dynamic characteristics of rock bursts. Compared to other forms of brittle rock failure, dynamic characteristics are the primary trait of rock bursts. The dynamic characteristics manifest in two aspects. Ejection characteristics: When a rock burst occurs, ejection and impact phenomena typically happen, meaning that the ejected rock pieces initially possess a certain velocity. Furthermore, the ejected rocks have a specific scatter angle during ejection; however, minor rock bursts may not exhibit ejection characteristics. Vibration characteristics: The amplitude of vibrations caused by rock bursts is related to the strength of the rock—when rock strength is low, the resulting vibrations are relatively weak, while higher rock strength correlates with stronger vibrations. Catastrophic rock bursts release substantial energy, leading to intense vibrations. These dynamic characteristics can result in the instantaneous collapse of tunnel surrounding rocks, complicating the protection of underground structures. (2) Initial surrounding rock stress characteristics in the rock burst occurrence area. A common feature of rock bursts is that the occurrence area generally has high initial surrounding rock stress, along with phenomena such as non-uniform stress distribution and localized stress concentration. Therefore, the likelihood of rock bursts increases with higher initial surrounding rock stress, complex stress changes, and greater tunnel depths. (3) Failure modes of rock bursts. Extensive engineering practice has demonstrated that the universal characteristic of rock burst damage is brittle fracture, with shearing failure and tensile failure being the primary forms of rock burst destruction. (4) Stochastic characteristics of rock bursts. Currently, there is no precise understanding of the mechanisms and conditions leading to rock bursts. Existing engineering practices and research largely rely on statistical methods for rock burst prediction, resulting in inherent randomness in the scale, timing, and location of rock bursts.

Tunnels, as significant structures, are widely utilized in highway engineering in the "three high and four active" southwestern region. However, during construction in extremely complex geological environments, they inevitably encounter typical underground engineering geological hazards such as collapses, rock bursts, large deformations, and water inrushes, which severely constrain safe and efficient construction, increasing economic costs and management pressures. Conducting risk assessments for tunnel geological hazards and implementing effective emergency response measures are of vital importance for disaster prevention and mitigation in tunnel projects. 

Based on research on the engineering geological conditions of the southwestern mountainous area, this paper analyzes the types and characteristics of geological hazards encountered during the construction of highway tunnels in this region. On this basis, the Mudashan Tunnel, a representative deeply buried long tunnel in the southwestern mountainous area, is selected as the research subject. The geological hazards during the construction process of this tunnel are identified, and the risk categories are determined. Subsequently, a risk evaluation index system for geological hazards in tunnel construction is established, and the weight of the indices is determined. A risk evaluation model based on index scoring methods is constructed, and this model is used to evaluate the geological hazard risks of the Mudashan Tunnel. Finally, emergency response measures for geological hazards in mountainous highway tunnels are proposed.

The main conclusions of this paper are as follows: 
(1) The southwestern region is located at the southeastern edge of the Eurasian Plate, adjacent to the Pacific Plate and the Indian Plate. Significant active faults are developed within the area, with strong neotectonic movements and frequent seismic activities, which are structurally controlled and lead to the formation of longitudinal canyon landforms. Most engineering structures traversing this area are tunnels. The extremely complex geological environment is a core issue faced during tunnel construction in the southwestern mountainous region and is also an intrinsic factor that causes collapses, rock bursts, water inrushes, and large deformations in tunnel construction. 

(2) The Mudashan Tunnel is situated at the junction of the Songpan-Ganzi orogenic belt and the western edge of the Yangtze Platform, crossing 17 major faults, with engineering geological characteristics such as significant burial depth, substantial lithological variation, complex geological structures, and developed groundwater. These geological characteristics result in a high risk level for water inrushes and large deformations, with high risk levels for collapses and rock bursts in the construction of the Mudashan Tunnel.

(3) Based on the risk characteristics of highway tunnels in the southwestern mountainous area, a risk assessment method for tunnels in this region is established, namely the index scoring method. The evaluation index system of the index scoring method comprises 18 scoring indicators categorized into geological factors, design factors, and construction factors. By determining the indicator weights, this method is applied to assess the risks of large deformations, collapses, water inrushes, and rock bursts in the Mudashan Tunnel, yielding evaluation conclusions that correspond to the types and levels of hazards that occurred during its construction. This indicates that the method is applicable for safety risk assessment of mountain highway tunnel construction.

(4) Based on clarifying the mechanisms and manifestation characteristics of collapses, rock bursts, water inrushes, and large deformations, the phases of emergency rescue for tunnel engineering geological hazards are divided into: preparation phase, implementation phase, and rescue summary phase. In conjunction with the entire process of sudden incidents—pre-event, event, during-event, and post-event—emergency response ideas and a categorized and graded emergency management mechanism are proposed for preemptive prevention, emergency rescue, and later disposal.

(5) For sudden geological hazards such as collapses, rock bursts, and water inrushes, it is proposed that the preemptive prevention phase adhere to the principle of "investigate before excavation." For gradual geological hazards characterized by large deformations, emphasis is placed on emergency preparedness, utilizing diverse monitoring means, and timely warnings based on monitoring results.

The classification and characteristic analysis of rockburst phenomena can be elucidated from multiple dimensions. Typically, based on practical engineering operations and field survey results, rockbursts are categorized according to their modes of failure, extent of impact, and preventive measures. Specifically, rockbursts can be classified into the following types based on fracture characteristics: relaxation-type, explosive ejection-type, and blast ejection-type.

Relaxation-type rockbursts are characterized by the surrounding rock exhibiting block-like, plate-like, or flake-like fractures, producing relatively soft sounds upon fracturing, occasionally resembling cracking noises. In these rockbursts, a small portion of rock (blocks or flakes) has already detached from the parent rock, but the ejection distance is minimal; fragments from the roof rockburst primarily fall directly downwards, while debris from the floor rockburst accumulates on-site, with most fragments still connected to the original rock, making separation from the rock wall difficult.

Explosive ejection-type rockbursts are defined by the complete detachment of rock fragments (flakes) from the parent rock, resulting in distinct rockburst craters. During such occurrences, the explosive sound resembles gunfire, and the ejected rock fragments (flakes) typically do not exceed one-third of a cubic meter in volume, with diameters ranging from 5 to 10 centimeters, varying in size and shape, including fist-sized blocks and the ejection of powdered rock material. The primary hazard is that the ejected rock fragments (flakes) can cause personal injury, while the impact on machinery and tunnel structures is relatively minor.

Blast ejection-type rockbursts are accompanied by a significant number of large boulders being ejected, accompanied by a loud noise, similar to the explosion of artillery shells. The volume of the ejected rocks can vary from several cubic meters to dozens of cubic meters, with ejection distances ranging from several meters to 10 to 20 meters. This type of rockburst causes damage to machinery and support structures (even though the vibrations themselves do not lead to structural failure).

Tunnel rockbursts can be classified based on their occurrence relative to different stages of tunnel excavation operations. The main categories are as follows:

1. Instantaneous rockbursts: These occur in a short time after the rock mass is excavated and exposed, typically during the advancement of the excavation face or within a few minutes to hours post-excavation. This type of rockburst is due to the rapid unloading of the rock mass (i.e., the stress release caused by excavation), where the internal stress of the rock exceeds its load-bearing capacity, leading to rock fracturing or even ejection. Instantaneous rockbursts reflect a sudden change in the stress state of the rock mass and are the most common type of rockburst.

2. Delayed rockbursts: These occur a longer time after excavation is completed, potentially several days, weeks, or even months later. Characteristics of this rockburst include occurrences at the surface or near-surface layers of the rock mass, primarily associated with the prolonged presence of the rock in a high-stress environment, as well as interactions between the rock and surrounding environment (such as water infiltration, temperature variations, etc.) leading to gradual reduction in rock strength or accumulation of stress to critical levels.

3. Periodic rockbursts: This refers to rockbursts that occur periodically during the tunnel excavation and support process. Such rockbursts may be related to the excavation method, construction pace, and periodic changes in the rock stress state. For instance, during continuous excavation and supporting operations, the possibility of rockbursts may increase during specific periodic stages due to stress adjustment and redistribution.

4. Stress adjustment-induced rockbursts: These rockbursts occur during the process of stress redistribution and adjustment in the rock during excavation. As the tunnel excavation progresses, the unexcavated rock mass will bear more load, resulting in changes in the internal stress state of the rock mass, which may accumulate sufficient stress in certain areas to trigger a rockburst. This type of rockburst can occur at any time after excavation and is closely related to the stress redistribution within the rock mass.

Factors Influencing Rock Burst: The occurrence of rock bursts in tunnels is influenced by multiple factors, including the physical and mechanical properties of the rock itself, as well as external environmental conditions and construction techniques. Understanding these influencing factors helps to implement appropriate preventive and control measures during tunnel design and construction.

Mechanical Properties of Rock: The physical and mechanical properties of rock, such as compressive strength, tensile strength, and elastic modulus, directly impact the occurrence of rock bursts. Generally, rocks with high compressive strength and significant brittleness are more susceptible to rock bursts.

Structural Characteristics of Rock: The distribution, density, and orientation of structural features (such as fractures, joints, and faults) within the rock also affect the likelihood of rock bursts. Rock masses with developed structural features and unfavorable orientations are more likely to experience rock bursts.

Stress State: The magnitude, direction, and distribution characteristics of in-situ stresses are critical factors influencing rock burst occurrences. In environments with high in-situ stress, especially when local stresses approach or exceed the compressive strength of the rock, the likelihood of rock bursts increases.

Tunnel Construction Methods: The excavation techniques used in tunneling, including blasting, can redistribute stresses in the surrounding rock mass, thereby increasing the risk of rock bursts.

Tunnel Depth: The greater the depth of the tunnel, the higher the surface loads and in-situ stresses typically encountered, which may subject the rock mass to greater stresses, increasing the risk of rock bursts.

Geological Conditions: The genesis, type (such as granite, metamorphic rock, etc.), and integrity of the rock mass affect the likelihood of rock bursts. Regions with complex geological conditions generally have a higher risk of rock bursts.

Hydrogeological Conditions: The presence and flow state of groundwater can alter the physical and mechanical properties of the rock, affecting the stress state and, consequently, the occurrence of rock bursts.

Environmental Factors: Environmental conditions such as temperature and humidity may indirectly influence the properties of the rock and the stress state, thereby affecting the occurrence of rock bursts.

Tunnel construction rockburst refers to the phenomenon where the stress equilibrium state of the original rock mass structure is disrupted due to tunnel excavation. When the accumulated elastic strain energy within the rock mass significantly exceeds the energy required for rock failure, a violent release of elastic potential energy occurs during the stress redistribution process. This leads to the explosion of the rock mass near the tunnel excavation profile and the detachment of rock fragments from the rock mass, resulting in a dynamic failure phenomenon. The rockburst disaster structure refers to the condition where the tunnel excavation exposes the previously intact rock mass, disrupting its stress equilibrium state. The accumulated high elastic strain energy is violently released during the stress redistribution, causing the explosion of the rock mass near the tunnel excavation outline, leading to the detachment and collapse of rock fragments, thus forming the rock mass associated with tunnel construction rockbursts. The rockburst disaster structures include: (1) deep-seated brittle intact rock mass under high geostress conditions; (2) brittle intact rock mass at points of abrupt terrain change at the tunnel surface.

During tunnel construction, deformation and failure of the surrounding rock is a common geological hazard, which can result in significant deformation, collapse, and fracturing of the surrounding rock mass and the overall area. In severe cases, it can even lead to dangerous phenomena such as rockbursts in hard surrounding rock formations. The occurrence of rockbursts during tunnel construction is quite frequent and often happens in areas with insufficient groundwater. Rockbursts manifest primarily as phenomena such as ejection, splitting, and flaking, and in more severe cases, they can trigger earthquakes and other serious geological hazards, posing a serious threat to human safety. Rockbursts occur without warning; sometimes, rocks may fall, while at other times, they may emit a cracking sound before remaining suspended temporarily. In the absence of appropriate support measures, there is a significant safety risk. Rockbursts typically occur at newly excavated faces and surrounding areas. Additionally, secondary rockbursts may also occur in certain locations. To mitigate the severity of hazards caused by rockbursts, effective prevention and control measures should be implemented, as follows: (1) Altering the properties of the surrounding rock. During tunnel construction, softening the surrounding rock can be achieved by drilling and injecting water or spraying near the tunnel walls, which can help slow down or eliminate the severity of rockbursts. (2) Improving the stress conditions of the surrounding rock. This is primarily done by reducing the stress in the surrounding rock to lower than its strength, thereby preventing rockbursts. In tunnel construction, smooth blasting techniques can be utilized, following a "short advance, multiple cycles" approach to minimize disturbances to the surrounding rock and improve its stress state; additionally, selecting the correct excavation cross-section can also help improve the stress state of the surrounding rock. (3) Strengthening support and implementing advanced support measures. Effective combinations of various reinforcement strategies or support methods should be employed to prevent rockbursts.

During tunnel engineering construction, it is easy to encounter hazards related to surrounding rock deformation and failure, which can trigger deformation-induced collapses and fractures, as well as give rise to additional hazards in the surrounding rock mass. The phenomenon of rock bursts often occurs during tunnel construction, along with insufficient groundwater presence. Currently, the principal forms of these hazards include spalling, splitting, and block falls. If rock bursts are severe, they can easily lead to seismic geological hazards, posing threats to public safety. Rock bursts occur without any warning, resulting in falling rocks, loud explosive sounds, and sudden collapses. Without appropriate supportive measures, safety issues are readily exacerbated. Rock bursts predominantly occur in newly excavated faces and their surrounding areas. 

To further mitigate the hazards produced by rock bursts, effective measures for optimization and control are needed, including: (1) Altering the properties of the surrounding rock. Typically, during tunnel construction, water injection or spraying is performed near the tunnel walls at the working face to soften the surrounding rock, effectively managing the entire process. (2) Improving the stress within the surrounding rock effectively. It is essential to ensure that the stress in the surrounding rock is less than its strength to effectively manage rock burst occurrences. During tunnel construction, smooth blasting techniques can be employed to significantly reduce disturbances in the surrounding rock, thereby improving the stress conditions of the surrounding rock mass while choosing an appropriate excavation cross-section to control the entire process. (3) Reinforcement and advance support involve selecting suitable methods for support during application to effectively control rock burst situations, thereby ensuring safer construction practices.

Rockburst is a typical geohazard under high geo-stress conditions, posing a significant threat to the safety of underground construction personnel and equipment. With the rapid development of water conservancy, transportation, and mining sectors in our country, deep excavation in high geo-stress environments is increasing, making the prevention and control of rockbursts increasingly prominent, becoming an essential issue in the field of geohazard prevention for deep underground engineering. Rockburst prediction is a crucial component of rockburst prevention and control. Accurate rockburst prediction aids in taking appropriate engineering measures in design and construction to reduce or avoid losses caused by rockburst disasters. However, due to the complex mechanisms of rockbursts, predicting them is quite challenging. Currently, a simple grading method is generally used for rockburst prediction in engineering practice, but this method often leads to results that deviate significantly from actual conditions since it does not account for the combined effects of various factors.

In response to the issues currently present in rockburst prediction, this paper conducts systematic research in the following areas: (1) Taking the Cangling Tunnel as an example, a systematic predictive analysis of its rockburst was carried out using traditional strength theory methods, discussing the problems of existing methods. (2) Addressing the limitations of traditional strength theory, considering the characteristics of rockbursts, the particle swarm optimization algorithm was used to optimize the generalized regression neural network, constructing an objective rockburst prediction model. This model was employed to predict rockbursts for two deep underground projects, the Cangling Tunnel and the Jinping II hydropower station, explaining the characteristics and limitations of this approach. (3) Given the continuity of rockburst analysis data and the discrete nature of rockburst classifications, combined with field survey results and domestic and international engineering cases, rough set theory was utilized to distinguish the importance of influence factors and objectively quantify evaluations. (4) Based on multi-objective programming principles, a rough set-ideal point rockburst prediction model was constructed, utilizing the results of rough set theory analysis and the ideal point method. The model's correctness and applicability were verified through predictions for the Cangling Tunnel and Jinping II hydropower station. (5) From an information fusion perspective, a similar rough set-ideal point rockburst prediction model was established, which was also validated with the two aforementioned engineering examples. (6) A comparative analysis among the rough set-ideal point model, rough set-evidence theory model, and fuzzy mathematical methods model was conducted to evaluate the advantages and disadvantages of each method and their predictive effectiveness.

Through the research on the above content, the following innovative results were obtained: (1) The rockburst prediction results for the Cangling Tunnel indicated that, compared to a standard BP neural network and standard generalized regression neural network, the particle swarm optimization-generalized regression neural network model provided stable outputs and accurate predictions. However, the model produced errors when predicting rockbursts for the Jinping II hydropower station's exploratory boreholes, indicating certain limitations in its applicability. (2) The analysis results of rough set theory showed that stress concentration has the greatest impact on rockbursts, while the energy storage conditions of the rock mass have a moderate influence, and the brittleness factors of the rock mass have a relatively minor effect. (3) The rockburst prediction results for the exploratory boreholes of the Cangling Tunnel and Jinping II hydropower station demonstrated that the rough set-ideal point model provided correct predictions and had a higher accuracy than the analytic hierarchy process-ideal point model and equal-weight-ideal point model. (4) The rockburst prediction results for the exploratory boreholes of the Cangling Tunnel and Jinping II hydropower station indicated that the rough set-evidence theory model predictions were correct and had higher accuracy than the two other evidence theory rockburst prediction models established through arbitrary specifications. (5) The rough set-ideal point rockburst prediction model, the rough set-evidence theory rockburst prediction model, and the fuzzy mathematical rockburst prediction model all demonstrated similar overall prediction levels; however, the rough set-ideal point model and the rough set-evidence theory model were more capable of reflecting the trends of rockburst development, suggesting that both are slightly superior to the fuzzy mathematical model.

The definition of rock burst varies among scholars worldwide, with different terms used such as rock burst, coal bump, dynamic ground pressure, rock ejection, and mining earthquakes. However, they all share a common feature of uncontrollable sudden failure of surrounding rock masses and the abrupt release of high strain energy. Rock bursts typically manifest as dynamic phenomena characterized by rock pieces being ejected, substantial rock collapses, or mining-induced seismic events, leading to severe damage to excavation sites, equipment destruction, and personnel casualties. In China, the term "rock burst" is predominantly used in hard rock metal mines, tunnels, and hydropower caverns, while in coal mining, it is more commonly referred to as dynamic ground pressure. Rock bursts are sudden events, often occurring near tunnel faces and sidewalls, and are distinctly different from roof collapses and landslides. Numerous factors can influence the occurrence of rock bursts, including in-situ stress, rock properties, rock mass structure, burial depth, topography, and human excavation activities. Analyzing the impact of these various factors is crucial for understanding the mechanisms of rock bursts, as well as for the reasonable prediction and prevention of such events.

Rockburst is one of the significant geological hazards in the mining of hard rock at great depths. To continually mitigate and fundamentally prevent rockbursts, extensive experimental and theoretical research has been conducted both domestically and internationally on their occurrence mechanisms, prediction methods, and prevention technologies. However, due to the extreme complexity of rockbursts, coupled with the diversity of geological conditions and the variability of engineering conditions in mines, this issue has not yet been overcome. Moreover, there is a scarcity of predictive research regarding rockburst tendencies in Jin Chuan Mine, the largest nickel production base in China. To address this issue, this paper, in conjunction with the major project of Jin Chuan Group Company "Rock Mechanics Research under High-Strength Mining Conditions in Jin Chuan Mine," proposes the topic of "Study on Rockburst Tendency in Deep Rocks of Jin Chuan Mine." It conducts an in-depth and systematic study on the occurrence mechanisms, prediction methods, and prevention technologies of rockbursts. The main research work and conclusions are summarized as follows: 

1. The types of rockbursts and their formation mechanisms, as well as prediction methods for rockburst tendencies, are analyzed. Three main theories of the rockburst mechanism are proposed: strength theory, energy theory, and impact tendency theory; and three primary prediction methods for rockbursts are introduced: predictions based on rock mechanics theory, field predictions, and predictions based on mathematical methods. 

2. A large number of typical rock samples were selected from the Jin Chuan second mining area. Uniaxial compressive strength tests, tensile strength tests, typical rock deformation tests under unloading conditions, and full stress-strain curve tests were conducted on four types of rock samples to determine their basic physical and mechanical parameters, energy impact indices (WCF), and elastic strain energy indices (WET). The rockburst tendency is predicted from an energy perspective through criteria based on energy impact indices and elastic strain energy indices. Additionally, based on the characteristics of significant burial depth and high geological stress in the deep mines of Jin Chuan, the author has deduced the geological stress values at a depth of 1000 meters underground in the mine area, building on previous studies of geological stress in the Jin Chuan second mining area. The rockburst tendency is also predicted from a stress perspective, integrating three major stress criteria: the Russene criterion, Barton criterion, and Hoek criterion.

3. Regarding the probability of rockburst occurrence, the paper further discusses the characteristics of rockbursts and their influencing factors. It proposes principles for controlling rockbursts and discusses control measures in detail during the study of rockburst mitigation. 

The field investigation and theoretical prediction work conducted for the topic of rockburst tendency in deep rocks of Jin Chuan Mine provides a reference and decision-making basis for mining design. The developed prediction and control theories and prevention measures for rockburst tendency in deep mines hold significant theoretical importance, as well as practical value and guidance for the production practices in deep well mining in China to prevent and mitigate rockburst disasters.

Rockburst is a dynamic destabilization phenomenon that occurs during the excavation of underground chambers in regions of high in-situ stress, characterized by the sudden release of strain energy leading to brittle failure of the surrounding rock mass. Rockbursts manifest in hard, brittle rock masses with substantial elastic deformation energy reserves, where excavation alters the stress distribution, causing an increase in surrounding rock stress and further energy concentration. Under the influence of surrounding rock stress, tensile and shear brittle failure occurs, accompanied by noise and vibrations that dissipate a portion of the elastic strain energy while the remaining energy transforms into kinetic energy. This transition facilitates the shift from static equilibrium to dynamic instability in the rock mass, resulting in the detachment of rock blocks or fragments, which are then violently propelled in the direction of the open space due to effective elastic energy release. This process is a progressive sequence characterized by "splitting into slabs, shearing into blocks, and the ejection of fragments." In regions of high in-situ stress, significant elastic strain energy accumulates within the rock mass. During or shortly after the excavation process, stress redistribution occurs, leading to stress concentration near the tunnel walls. When this concentrated stress exceeds the rock mass's failure strength, the strain energy is suddenly released, causing violent brittle instability and failure. The resulting surrounding rock often experiences explosive spalling and dislodgment, with rock blocks being violently ejected or thrown, frequently accompanied by noise. This form of rock mass failure is termed rockburst. Minor rockbursts may only result in spalling of rock flakes without ejection, while severe rockbursts can violently expel large boulders, with some events capable of ejecting tons of rock blocks and fragments, and can even be measured as a certain level of seismic activity. Rockbursts represent a significant geological hazard in underground constructions such as mining tunnels, transportation tunnels, underground powerhouses in hydropower projects, and water conveyance tunnels. They pose direct threats to the safety of construction personnel and equipment, impact project timelines, and have become one of the global challenges in underground engineering.

Rockburst is a complex dynamic phenomenon that exhibits significant variability in terms of scale, degree of destruction, occurrence time, and location. From the perspective of the energy release phenomenon, rockbursts display sudden destructive characteristics; from the viewpoint of surrounding rock stress, they encompass processes of concentration, exceedance, failure, and transfer; and from the angle of energy variation, they involve processes of accumulation and dissipation. However, there are some common characteristics: 

1. Stress characteristics in rockburst-prone areas. Numerous rockburst cases indicate that these regions generally possess high geostress, with localized stress concentration and non-uniform stress distribution. Therefore, the likelihood of rockbursts occurring is greater in areas where geostress is high and complex, as well as in those at greater depths. 

2. Dynamic characteristics of rockbursts. Most rockbursts are accompanied by noise, sudden collapses, ejection, or impact phenomena. Current research suggests that this is a dynamic manifestation of energy conversion in the rock mass. The dynamic characteristics of rockbursts pose challenges for the protection of underground engineering, as the impact energy from a rockburst can lead to sudden collapses of the excavation space or significant ejection of large rock fragments.

3. Destructive nature of rockbursts. Brittle fracturing is a common characteristic of rockburst destruction. The failures associated with rockbursts primarily consist of tension failures and shear failures, which have been confirmed through microscopic analysis of rockburst fracture surfaces.

4. Geological characteristics of rockburst occurrence areas. Rockbursts typically occur in hard, high-strength rock masses, particularly near contact zones between rocks of differing strengths and geological structural zones. The structure of the rock mass (the type of surrounding rock, the angle between primary structural planes and geostress) plays a controlling role in the occurrence and intensity of rockbursts.

5. Engineering characteristics of rockbursts. The frequency of rockburst occurrences increases with the size of underground excavations; as underground chambers become more complex, the greater the interaction, the higher the probability of rockbursts.

6. Randomness of rockburst occurrences. Current research indicates that no combination of natural and engineering factors can unequivocally result in a rockburst. Predictions of rockbursts are merely statistical outcomes, and both the timing, range, and specific location of rockburst occurrences exhibit a certain degree of randomness.

Rockburst is a frequent and sudden geological hazard that occurs during the construction of large tunnels. Research on rockbursts has significant practical implications for tunnel surveying design, construction organization, and safety production. Understanding the mechanism of rockbursts is essential for the development of prediction and control technologies. However, due to the inadequacies of existing theories, the prediction systems and control technologies based on these theories are still unable to accurately forecast and effectively mitigate rockburst disasters. 

Static rock mechanics theory plays an important role in rockburst research, but it cannot fully explain the entire mechanism of rockbursts. During the drilling and blasting excavation process, the sequence of blast initiation for each layer, the instantaneous unloading of excavation profiles following the detonation of surrounding holes, and the various high-amplitude, steep-wavefront stress waves generated by the rockburst event, along with their superposition effects, exert a significant disturbance on the surrounding rock mass. This is particularly relevant for rock masses under biaxial compression, with one side being unsupported, and having the potential for failure due to pre-existing explosions.

Although the surrounding rock may not experience macro-damage from a single instance of these dynamic loads, the repeated disturbances from these stress waves can exacerbate cumulative damage and degrade the local stress environment at the micro- and mesoscopic scales. Ultimately, this can lead to large-scale instantaneous dynamic crack propagation accompanied by rapid release of intercrystalline and intergranular transient strain energy, causing the surrounding rock to destabilize in the form of a rockburst. Therefore, it is essential to systematically investigate the dynamic mechanics of rockbursts.

Deep mining is an essential path for the future development of hard rock mines. One of the key scientific issues that must be addressed in the development process of hard rock mines is the rock burst disaster frequently encountered during deep mining. In addressing the global scientific challenge of rock burst mechanisms, a scientific analysis is conducted on the energy storage characteristics of deep surrounding rock, the high-stress environment, and the full-path stress conditions during mining. It is found that the problem fundamentally belongs to the mechanics of static stress combined with dynamic perturbation in hard rock media. Based on this understanding, a comprehensive analysis of typical rock burst cases from both domestic and international sources is performed, examining the necessary conditions for rock burst genesis, including intact brittle rock mass, high geostress, and dynamic disturbances. The dynamic-static combined mechanical mechanisms of rock bursts in deep hard rock mines and recent research advancements are elaborated upon. In specific research, using hard rock materials, the mechanical mechanisms of rock bursts are studied from both static stress combined with unload disturbances and static stress combined with load disturbances. Additionally, considering the spatial structural effects of deep engineering excavation, the failure characteristics of strain-type rock bursts in the walls of deep circular chambers are investigated under static stress combined with stress adjustments and static stress combined with dynamic disturbances, clarifying the different types of dynamic-static combined loading mechanisms. On this basis, a rock burst dynamic criterion and control approach based on the dynamic-static energy index are proposed, providing new ideas and methods for the exploration of rock burst mechanisms through in-depth research on the combined effects of static and dynamic loading.

Rockburst and dynamic pressure are phenomena that do not exist in nature; they are entirely triggered by human activities such as deep underground space utilization and deep mineral resource extraction projects, both classified as typical geological hazards associated with deep engineering. Due to historical reasons, the concepts of rockburst and dynamic pressure have been long confused with one another. This article provides a detailed overview of the early domestic research developments in the field of rockbursts and dynamic pressure, systematically analyzing the differences between the two. 

There are fundamental distinctions in terms of research objects (the bearing capacity, energy storage, and release capabilities, as well as brittle elasticity of hard rock and coal), stress conditions (geostress and disturbance stress), and boundary conditions (excavation and mining methods and procedures, disturbance range, and timing). In terms of observable phenomena, defined objects, research subjects, occurrence conditions, industry sectors, engineering construction methods, objectives and requirements of construction, supporting characteristics, triggering mechanisms, tendencies, classification types, grading levels, and evaluation methods, significant differences also exist between rockburst and dynamic pressure. 

In conclusion, rockburst and dynamic pressure are two parallel types of dynamic geological damage phenomena, and there is no hierarchical relationship between them. Based on a comprehensive reference to prior studies, distinct definitions and connotations for each phenomenon are proposed: rockburst is defined as the ejection, bursting, or collapse of hard rock occurring in deep tunnels, underground mine entries, and pillar locations, accompanied by varying degrees of noise; dynamic pressure is defined as the phenomenon of coal throw occurring in deep coal mines, releasing varying levels of kinetic energy, often accompanied by vibrations, loud noises, air blasts, or shock waves in severe cases. From the perspective of unified naming regarding coal dynamic impact damage phenomena, it is suggested to replace the concept of dynamic pressure with coal impact. 

Building on this, seven points of understanding in the study of rockbursts and dynamic pressure are elaborated. Finally, the critical mechanism issues of rockburst and dynamic pressure are discussed from three aspects: research objects, stress conditions, and boundary conditions. This involves studying rockburst and dynamic pressure from the perspective of static-dynamic (or dynamic-static) combined loading mechanics, conforming to the overall force path of deep geological body destruction, while also addressing the transition from static to dynamic in terms of energy conservation. In the mechanism analysis, prediction and forecasting, monitoring and alarm systems, and regulatory prevention and control of rockbursts and dynamic pressure, it is essential to scientifically understand the logical and dialectical relationships between various influencing factors.

As mining depth increases and the conditions of ore and mining environment deteriorate, rockburst has increasingly become an inevitable and frequently occurring dynamic hazard during the extraction process in underground metal mines. Based on a comprehensive review of the current status, influencing factors, typical characteristics, types and grades, as well as the genesis process of rockbursts in domestic and international underground metal mines, this study discusses and analyzes the prediction and prevention techniques for rockbursts in shafts and stope areas. The results indicate that: (1) Globally, rockburst occurrences can be generally classified into six regions; currently, over 20 underground metal mines in China have experienced or are undergoing rockburst disasters, primarily distributed in an S-shaped pattern from Northeast to Southwest along the nearshore coastline, with the number of mines experiencing rockbursts increasing over the past 30 years; (2) The influencing factors of rockbursts in underground metal mines can be categorized into three classes: Class I (intrinsic determining factors), Class II (external triggering factors), and Class III (aggravating factors), where each category of factors is both independent and interrelated; (3) Based on engineering types, rockbursts can be divided into shaft rockbursts and stope rockbursts. The engineering protection for shaft rockbursts should generally adhere to basic principles that combine active and passive support, rigid and flexible support, and transition from single to multi-method coordinated support. Meanwhile, the prevention and control of stope rockbursts is still in the exploratory research phase, where it is suggested that measures can be taken in terms of mining methods and processes, stope layout, and extraction sequence, with backfill mining being the primary direction and trend for addressing ground pressure disasters such as rockbursts in deep well mining; (4) The prevention and control of rockbursts in underground metal mines should be integrated throughout the entire mining process. Establishing a long-term monitoring and feedback mechanism and developing corresponding industry technical standards are urgent necessities in this field. Finally, the study discusses and analyzes the challenges faced in the research related to rockbursts in underground metal mines and outlines key future research topics, which may provide references for the establishment of a rockburst prevention and control system and the design of mining engineering.
